title: Implement pure-Rust RS256 key-pair JWT signing and public-key fingerprint
description: Concrete forbidden-dependency-free crypto path for Snowflake key-pair auth. Use jsonwebtoken v10 with default-features=false, features=[rust_crypto,use_pem] (pure-Rust rsa+sha2 RS256; no OpenSSL, no ring signing, no Tokio). Load a PKCS#8 PEM private key (passphrase-protected supported) and sign an RS256 JWT with the EXACT Snowflake claim set: iss = "<ACCOUNT>.<USER>.SHA256:<fp>" (WITH the public-key fingerprint), sub = "<ACCOUNT>.<USER>" (NO fingerprint), iat, exp. ACCOUNT and USER are UPPERCASE; for the org-account form whose account segment contains a ".", replace it with "-". Fingerprint = SHA256:<base64(SHA-256 of the DER public key)> via sha2+base64. Snowflake caps effective JWT validity at 1 hour regardless of the requested exp, so cap exp at <=3600s AND support re-signing INSIDE the statement bracket so a query polled past ~1h does not lose auth mid-flight. Set X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: KEYPAIR_JWT and the bearer header. Precedent: this exact jsonwebtoken v10 rust_crypto+use_pem path already ships in fastmcp-server's jwt feature -- cite that, NOT mcp_agent_mail_rust (which uses the aws_lc_rs C-backed feature; do not copy it). Ref: docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/sql-api/authenticating (2026-06-24).
acceptance_criteria: RS256 sign+verify round-trip golden using a test PKCS#8 key; golden asserting the claim formats differ (iss = ACCOUNT.USER.SHA256:<fp> but sub = ACCOUNT.USER with no fp); uppercase + org-account "."->"-" account normalization test; SHA256:base64(DER pubkey) fingerprint matches the Snowflake format on a known key; exp<=3600 cap test; mid-flight re-sign path test (token re-issued inside a simulated long poll); X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type=KEYPAIR_JWT header set; passphrase-protected key load test; no secret in Debug/JSON (redaction test); cargo-tree proof jsonwebtoken rust_crypto pulls no openssl/ring-signing/tokio; structured JSON-line logs.
close_reason: RS256 key-pair JWT signer + public-key fingerprint implemented (commit cbd8129) in franken-snowflake-auth via jsonwebtoken(rust_crypto/use_pem). Verified GREEN: 'rch cargo check -p franken-snowflake-auth' compiles (5.77s); fingerprint present in lib.rs. Unblocks kdw.

title: Adopt Asupersync Budget/Outcome/CancelReason/capability semantics in core
description: Adopt the Asupersync Budget/Outcome/CancelKind/capability control plane in franken-snowflake-core and thread it to the edges. Build SnowflakeOutcome<T> on the four-valued Outcome (Ok/Err/Cancelled/Panicked) and preserve all four to the CLI/MCP edge, collapsing only at the policy boundary (exit code/MCP error/receipt status). Own the envelope contract types (OutcomeKind, DataSource), the error code ranges, and the central error registry mapping each code to default safe_next_commands/repair_commands. Model query Budget with deadline + poll quota + cost quota and meet() propagation to partition fetchers; the cost quota is ADVISORY (the enforceable ceiling is server-side STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS + result row caps) and a breach surfaces as Cancelled(CancelKind::CostBudget). Cancellation policy keys off reason.kind: CancelKind -- CancelReason is a STRUCT, not a flat enum (asupersync/src/types/cancel.rs). Route Deadline/CostBudget (budget exhaustion) and Timeout (explicit timeout combinator) to retry/degrade, User to remote-cancel + receipt, Shutdown to bounded drain, RaceLost/ParentCancelled to quiet drain. Read-only is enforced at the type level AT THE RIGHT LAYER: the pure planning/validation/SQL-compile path runs under cx_readonly() (= Cx<cap::None>, zero capabilities including NO IO); the transport layer runs under a narrowed Cx granting IO (+TIME/SPAWN) but NEVER REMOTE; only the write-intent ladder widens authority further. See docs/asupersync_leverage.md.
acceptance_criteria: unit tests that all four Outcome states survive to the envelope and map to correct exit codes (empty result = exit 0); error-registry completeness test (every code has a default recovery path); Budget meet() child-tightening propagation test; reason.kind / CancelKind -> policy mapping tests with Deadline and CostBudget routed distinctly from Timeout; a compile-time/test assertion that the pure planner layer holds cx_readonly() (no IO) while the transport layer holds a narrowed Cx with IO+TIME+SPAWN but NOT REMOTE, and that only the write-intent ladder can widen further; CostBudget breach maps to a distinct outcome_kind/exit code; structured JSON-line logs.

comments.author: claude-plan-review
comments.text:
Plan correction 2026-06-24 (verified against /data/projects/asupersync source):

- CancelReason is a STRUCT, not a flat enum. Match reason.kind: CancelKind (asupersync/src/types/cancel.rs). A budgeted query that runs out of time surfaces as CancelKind::Deadline (and a cost-quota breach as CancelKind::CostBudget), NOT Timeout (which is the explicit timeout-combinator path). The policy table must route Deadline/CostBudget explicitly (retry or degrade). Update the acceptance criterion accordingly.
- cx_readonly() returns Cx<cap::None> -- ZERO capabilities, including no IO (asupersync/src/cx/cap.rs). So the acceptance criterion "read handlers cannot obtain IO/REMOTE/SPAWN" is wrong for the transport layer: the network read path DOES need a narrowed Cx with IO (and TIME/SPAWN) but never REMOTE. cx_readonly() fits only the pure planning/validation/SQL-compile layer. Re-word the read-only enforcement test: planner layer = cx_readonly(); transport layer = narrowed Cx with IO, REMOTE off; only the write-intent ladder widens further.
- cost quota is advisory; enforceable ceiling is server-side STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS + row caps. A CostBudget breach maps to a distinct outcome_kind/exit code.
- The submit POST is NOT retried by the client RetryPolicy (non-idempotent methods excluded). Our jittered backoff owns submit retries, made safe by requestId + retry=true.

See docs/asupersync_leverage.md (updated 2026-06-24).

title: Capture Snowflake SQL API protocol schemas and goldens
description: Capture Snowflake SQL API request/response schemas as typed Rust with serde: SubmitStatementRequest/Response, QueryStatus, QueryFailureStatus, ResultSet, ResultSetMetaData (rowType with type/scale/precision/nullable/length), PartitionInfo (rowCount/compressedSize/uncompressedSize; top-level numRows = total across partitions), StatementCancelResponse, the parameters object, session parameters, query tags, and idempotency fields (requestId + retry=true). Bindings are POSITIONAL and TYPED ({"1":{type,value}}, value ALWAYS a string) by hard rule; never string interpolation. Model the HTTP status codes as DISTINCT states (do not conflate 429 with 202): 200 completed (ResultSet); 202 running/accepted-async (QueryStatus; the poll-again signal, also returned when a sync statement exceeds ~45s); 408 statement timeout (STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS); 422 failure (QueryFailureStatus); 429 server overload/rate-limit (Retry-After NOT guaranteed). jsonv2 result encoding: every data cell is a JSON STRING (the per-type decode math lives in the frame bead z1o) — the schema layer must type cells as strings and never infer from JSON shape. Multi-statement: MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT (exact N, or 0=variable) -> statementHandles[]; bindings unsupported in that mode. The parameters object pins deterministic output (timezone, *_output_format, binary_output_format, use_cached_result, MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT); the nullable query param controls NULL rendering (null vs "null") and is unrelated to rowType[].nullable. Ship official-example golden fixtures so the schemas are exercised before any live account exists. Refs: docs.snowflake.com SQL API submitting-requests / handling-responses / submitting-multiple-statements / reference (2026-06-24).
acceptance_criteria: serde round-trip golden tests for every schema type against official SQL API example JSON; status-code routing test proving 200/202/408/422/429 deserialize to distinct typed states; requestId + retry=true fields present and serialized; cells typed as strings (jsonv2) at the schema layer; null-handling and rowType scale/precision parsing tests; positional-binding serialization test (value-as-string, 1-based keys); multi-statement MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT + statementHandles[] shape test and a bindings-refused-in-multi-statement test; parameters-object pinning test; PartitionInfo + numRows accounting test; deterministic key-sorted goldens with canonicalized volatile fields; structured JSON-line test logs per case.

comments.author: claude-plan-review
comments.text:
Plan correction 2026-06-24 (Snowflake-docs-validated). Extend the protocol schemas to cover facts the first draft missed:

- Status codes are distinct, not interchangeable: 200=completed (ResultSet), 202=running/accepted-async (QueryStatus; the poll-again signal, also returned when a sync statement exceeds ~45s), 408=statement timeout (STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS), 422=failure (QueryFailureStatus), 429=server overload/rate limit (Retry-After NOT guaranteed). Model 408 explicitly; do not conflate 429 with 202.
- Idempotency contract: submit POST is not idempotent on its own; a client requestId (UUID) + retry=true query param makes resubmit safe (returns prior result instead of re-executing). Capture both fields in SubmitStatementRequest.
- jsonv2 wire encoding: every data cell is a JSON STRING decoded per its rowType, not by JSON shape. NUMBER/FIXED = plain decimal (do NOT divide by 10^scale); BOOLEAN = "true"/"false"; DATE = days-since-epoch; TIME/TIMESTAMP_NTZ/LTZ = fractional epoch SECONDS; TIMESTAMP_TZ = "<epoch_sec.frac> <offset>" with offset = minutes+1440; BINARY = hex; VARIANT/OBJECT/ARRAY = embedded JSON. Docs are inconsistent on timestamp units, so the codec must be pinned by an empirical live golden.
- Multi-statement: gated by MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT (exact N, or 0=variable); response returns statementHandles[]; bindings are NOT supported in multi-statement mode.
- parameters object pins deterministic output (timezone, *_output_format, binary_output_format, use_cached_result, MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT). The nullable query param controls NULL rendering (null vs "null") and is unrelated to rowType[].nullable.

Refs: docs.snowflake.com SQL API submitting-requests, handling-responses, submitting-multiple-statements, reference (consulted 2026-06-24).

title: Implement statement submit, poll, partition fetch, and cancel lifecycle
description: Implement the statement lifecycle (submit -> poll -> partition fetch -> cancel) modeled as an Asupersync bracket so the remote cancel endpoint ALWAYS fires on drop/cancel and no Snowflake statement is orphaned. Concurrent partition fetch runs under one bounded Scope with child Budgets (meet()). Handle the distinct status codes: 200 completed; 202 still-running (poll the statement handle / statementStatusUrl; the not-yet-ready signal, also returned when a sync statement exceeds ~45s); 408 statement timeout (STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS) as a typed timeout outcome; 422 query failure; 429 rate-limiting (jittered exponential backoff; Retry-After is NOT guaranteed; distinct from 202, never a query-status code). Treat cancelled-during-connect as a DISTINCT receipt state (the asupersync TLS handshake is not cancel-safe; cancel mid-handshake drops the connection and no statement was submitted). Submit retry is OWNED here via custom jittered backoff keyed to a stable requestId + retry=true (the asupersync client RetryPolicy does NOT retry the non-idempotent POST; built-in retry only assists the idempotent GET poll/partition path; a safe resubmit returns the prior result rather than re-executing). gzip partitions (manual GzipDecompressor off content-encoding); positional typed bindings; multiple-statement refusal by default (when explicitly allowed: MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT + statementHandles[] fan-out, bindings refused). Provide a RESULT_SCAN re-fetch path passing the stored Snowflake query_id (keyed by normalized-plan hash), subject to Snowflake result-cache retention (~24h); LAST_QUERY_ID only addresses the session-immediate statement.
acceptance_criteria: submit/poll/cancel happy-path test on the testkit; cancel triggers the remote cancel endpoint (verified via mock) under bracket; partition fetch concurrency bounded by Scope with child Budgets; 200/202/408/422/429 each handled as distinct states (202 poll-loop; 429 jittered backoff WITHOUT relying on Retry-After; 408 typed timeout outcome; 422 failure); cancelled-during-connect distinct-receipt-state test; idempotent-resubmit test (requestId + retry=true returns the prior result, no re-execute); gzip partition decode; multiple-statement refusal by default + a MULTI_STATEMENT_COUNT fan-out test when allowed (bindings refused); RESULT_SCAN by stored query_id re-fetch test; deep concurrency races covered by the DPOR suite; structured JSON-line logs with statement_handle and query_id (redacted profile).
close_reason: Statement lifecycle complete (part1 c44e91f state machine + 0685e1ef full driver): submit, poll/await, partition fetch (gzip), and cancel via SQL API cancel endpoint on Asupersync cancellation, driven against the deterministic testkit mock. Verified GREEN: 'rch cargo check -p franken-snowflake-sqlapi'. Unblocks catalog/frame/export/.4/.8/mcp/tui.

comments.author: claude-plan-review
comments.text:
Plan correction 2026-06-24. Additions to the statement lifecycle (the 202/429 distinction and RESULT_SCAN-by-query_id are already captured -- good):

- Handle 408 (statement timeout, exceeded STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS) as a distinct typed timeout outcome, separate from a 422 query failure.
- Handle "cancelled during connect" as a distinct receipt state: the asupersync TLS handshake is not cancel-safe, so cancel mid-handshake means drop the connection -- not the same as a submitted-then-cancelled statement.
- Submit retry: the asupersync client RetryPolicy does NOT retry POST (non-idempotent). The lifecycle must own submit retries via custom jittered backoff, keyed to a stable requestId + retry=true so a resubmit returns the prior result instead of re-executing.
- 429: Retry-After is not guaranteed by Snowflake; use jittered exponential backoff and do not depend on the header.

Acceptance additions: 408 timeout outcome test; cancelled-during-connect receipt-state test; idempotent-resubmit (requestId+retry=true) test.

title: Plan optional Frankensearch text indexing for unstructured data
description: Design the optional text-indexing lane for Snowflake tables/views containing filings, transcripts, notes, JSON blobs, or other unstructured text. Frankensearch indexes selected text columns or exported chunks with provenance back to statement/query/dataset receipts, using ONLY the hash/lexical features (never fastembed/download, which pull ort/ONNX + openssl). Define a Reranker trait with a NO-OP default in the core search path; the pure-Rust frankensearch-rerank `native` cross-encoder (forbidden-dep-clean and cross-platform; runs on frankentorch tensors, no ort/ONNX) is a DEFERRED, feature-gated drop-in behind a `rerank` feature, added only if a long-text retrieval surface emerges. It is a reranker (one BERT forward per doc, ~45-120ms), so it is constrained to TOP-K (K<=50) refinement over long text columns -- never short-text catalog search or interactive ranking -- with caller-provided weights and a documented latency budget; never the fastembed-reranker/download path. Acceptance: design doc explains feature flags, rights propagation, indexing inputs, stable result handles, the Reranker-trait seam, and why each layer is optional.
acceptance_criteria: optional frankensearch indexing of unstructured text columns using ONLY the hash/lexical features (never fastembed/download); IndexBuilder add_document + TwoTierSearcher query test over fixture docs; a Reranker trait with a no-op default is defined and unit-tested (default leaves order unchanged); cargo-tree proof the offline indexing path pulls no tokio/reqwest/hyper/ort/onnx; if the optional rerank feature is wired, a cargo-tree proof it pulls no ort/onnx/openssl/tokio and a top-K-only enforcement test with a documented latency budget; feature-gated so the default build excludes both indexing and rerank; structured logs.

comments.author: claude-plan-review
comments.text:
Update 2026-06-24: frankensearch now has a PURE-RUST semantic reranker that does NOT need ort/ONNX. Crate frankensearch-rerank v0.2.2, feature "native", type NativeReranker -- a cross-encoder (ms-marco-MiniLM-L6-v2) reimplemented on pure-Rust frankentorch tensors (frankensearch commit e717b8b, 2026-06-23, which DELETED the old ort-based path). cargo-tree of frankensearch-rerank --features native is forbidden-dep-clean (no tokio/ort/onnx/openssl/reqwest; pure-Rust tokenizer via fancy-regex; no compiled C/C++) and cross-platform (no CUDA/Metal; portable `wide` SIMD; runs Linux/macOS/Windows).

Caveat: it is a RERANKER, not a retriever -- one full BERT forward per (query,doc) pair (~45-120ms/doc), so it only fits TOP-K (K<=50) refinement over LONG unstructured text columns, not short-text catalog search (lexical/fuzzy wins there) and not interactive ranking. Weights (model_f32.safetensors + tokenizer.json, ~90MB) are caller-provided; do NOT enable the fastembed-reranker/download path (reintroduces ort+openssl).

Plan decision: keep hash/lexical as the core. Define a Reranker trait with a NO-OP default; wire frankensearch-rerank `native` behind a `rerank` feature as a deferred drop-in, added only if a long-text retrieval surface emerges. Acceptance if adopted: cargo-tree proof the rerank feature pulls no ort/onnx/openssl/tokio; top-K-only enforced; latency budget documented.
